Forecasting UK Train Delays

Phyo Ung

March 2026

Introduction

Train delays can be frustrating for passengers. When a train does not arrive at the planned time, passengers may need to adjust their plans or schedules, which can cause inconvenience.

In this project, I analyse train delay data using the Prophet forecasting to identify patterns in the future train delays.

Data

The dataset used in this project is obtained from the Ofiice of Rail and Road. Table 3184 contains delay minutes for train, reported every four weeks and categorised by cause of delay.

## Rows: 2189 Columns: 17
## ── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## Delimiter: ","
## chr  (2): time period, train operating company name
## dbl (15): NR on TOC External, NR on TOC Network Management Other, NR on TOC ...
## 
## ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
## ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.

Data preparation

## # A tibble: 6 × 2
##   ds               y
##   <date>       <dbl>
## 1 2019-04-01 318005.
## 2 2019-04-29 345190.
## 3 2019-05-27 357393 
## 4 2019-06-24 389613.
## 5 2019-07-22 363160.
## 6 2019-08-19 368930.

Exploratory analysis

The plot shows a noticeable trend in the total delay minutes over time. There is a significant drop in train delay minutes during 2020, which may be due to the reduction in train services during the COVID-19 pandemic. As time progresses, the delay minutes begin to increase again.

Prophet model

The Prophet model is fitted to the train delay time series to forecast future delays by capturing trends and seasonal patterns in the data.

## Disabling weekly seasonality. Run prophet with weekly.seasonality=TRUE to override this.
## Disabling daily seasonality. Run prophet with daily.seasonality=TRUE to override this.

Forecast

The forecast below shows the predicted total delay minutes for future periods with uncertainty intervals.

Forecast plot

The Blue line gives the expected delay minutes, and the shaded area reflects uncertainty in the predictions. This provides an range with which future train delays may fall.

Forecast components

## Warning: `aes_string()` was deprecated in ggplot2 3.0.0.
## ℹ Please use tidy evaluation idioms with `aes()`.
## ℹ See also `vignette("ggplot2-in-packages")` for more information.
## ℹ The deprecated feature was likely used in the prophet package.
##   Please report the issue at <https://github.com/facebook/prophet/issues>.
## This warning is displayed once per session.
## Call `lifecycle::last_lifecycle_warnings()` to see where this warning was
## generated.

The components plot helps show the trend and any seasonal patterns identified by the model. This makes it easier to understand how Prophet builds the forecast.

Comparison model

The second Prophet model is fitted without yearly seasonality

The comparison model confirms the interpretation from the exploratory analysis. The forecast shows that the total minutes of train delay generally increase over time, reflecting the upward trend observed in the earlier plot.

Conclusion

This project analysed total train delay minutes using the Prophet forecasting model. The exploratory analysis showed a noticeable drop in delay minutes around 2020, likely due to the reduction in train services during the COVID-19 lockdown. After this period, delay minutes generally increased over time.

The Prophet model was then fitted to the data to forecast results shows that train delays may continue to follow an upward trend, reflecting the pattern in the historical data. The model capture underlying trends and seasonal patterns.

Overall, this project demonstrates how Prophet can be used for analysing data and generating forecasts. While the model provides useful insights, the forecasts are based solely on historical patterns and exclude weather condition, infrastructure improvements or unexpected disruptions. Future analysis can explore additional variables to gain deeper insights into train delay patterns.
